title: Taking an Up-Close Look at the Supermicro GB300 Super AI Station
gist: Supermicro's GB300 Super AI Station—showcased at Computex 2026—puts NVIDIA's GB300 Grace Blackwell chip (252GB GPU memory, 7.1 TB/s bandwidth, 400GbE ConnectX-8) in a $125,000 liquid-cooled desktop/5U rack-convertible form factor, making it the only way to access GB300 compute outside a full server rack deployment.

insights.claim: The Supermicro GB300 Super AI Station is priced at approximately $125,000 and provides access to NVIDIA's GB300 Grace Blackwell accelerator in a desktop form factor—the only way to get GB300 compute smaller than a full server.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: At $125K, DGX Station targets enterprise AI development teams needing local GB300 access; watch whether adoption accelerates or teams prefer cloud-based GB300 access.

insights.claim: The DGX Station ships with only 7 of 8 HBM3e stacks enabled on the GB300 chip—providing 252GB of GPU memory and 7.1 TB/s memory bandwidth—as NVIDIA de-rates the part for this form factor.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: 252GB/7.1TB/s defines the GB300 desktop tier; the gap vs. full-server GB300 configurations signals NVIDIA reserving full silicon for higher-margin server deployments.
insights.quote: DGX Station systems ship with only 7 of the 8 HBM3e stacks on the chip enabled, for a total of 252GB of GPU memory and a total memory bandwidth of 7.1TB/second.
insights.locator: only 7 of the 8 HBM3e stacks on the chip enabled

insights.claim: The GB300 chip in the DGX Station draws 1,600W requiring liquid cooling with cold plates on all critical components; Supermicro's front-to-back radiator configuration also enables the system to be rack-mounted as a 5U server.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: 1.6kW desktop power draw requires a dedicated circuit; the 5U rack-mount option gives enterprises flexibility but is not space-efficient vs. HGX-based servers.
insights.locator: With most of the system's 1600 Watt TDP

insights.claim: The DGX Station includes two 400GbE ports via NVIDIA's ConnectX-8, providing high-bandwidth networking for multi-system interconnect or fast data center backhaul.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: 400GbE connectivity positions DGX Station as a distributed training node, not just a standalone workstation—lowers the barrier to multi-node GB300 clusters at the desk or in the rack.

themes.theme: OEM constraints and GPU design limitations

insights.claim: NVIDIA provides DGX Station OEMs with the critical motherboard and processors while constraining OEM design to specific requirements (port availability, power), resulting in nearly identical core component layouts across all DGX Station offerings—limiting OEM differentiation.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: NVIDIA captures most value from the DGX Station platform while OEMs compete primarily on system integration and support; reinforces NVIDIA's hardware monetization model.
insights.quote: NVIDIA has kept OEMs on a relatively short leash with DGX Station systems.
insights.locator: NVIDIA has kept OEMs on a relatively short leash

insights.claim: The GB300 chip is compute-optimized and cannot output graphics; a separate RTX PRO GPU card must be installed to enable workstation-grade graphics beyond basic BMC video output—adding cost and PCIe slot dependency.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: GB300 workstation deployments require a secondary GPU for visualization; adds cost and complexity for mixed compute-plus-graphics workflows.
insights.quote: The GB300 is not graphics-capable due to its compute-optimized design, so an RTX PRO card must be installed to enable graphics beyond BMC video.

insights.claim: Windows versions of DGX Station systems will become available later in 2026 under 'DGX Station for Windows' branding, expanding beyond the current NVIDIA DGX OS (custom Ubuntu) offering.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Windows support expands DGX Station's addressable market to enterprise environments with Windows infrastructure; may accelerate adoption in finance and enterprise AI teams.
insights.locator: OEMs begin selling Windows versions under the DGX Station for Windows branding
